title: The Difference Between Forgiveness and Freedom
researcherTopic: polyvagal theory, neuroception, the social engagement system
metaDescription: Distinguish between the cognitive act of forgiveness and the somatic state of freedom in healing C-PTSD. Learn to reclaim your nervous system from the past.
imageDescription: A delicate piece of pottery, shattered and then meticulously repaired with shimmering gold, rests on a rustic wooden surface. Warm, gentle morning light illuminates the golden seams, highlighting the beauty of its restoration and resilience.
finalH2: From Forgiveness to Freedom: A Shift in Gaze
htmlContent:
I've sat with hundreds of people as they wrestle with the ghost of forgiveness, this pervasive cultural mandate that suggests peace is only available once you have cognitively absolved those who harmed you. Yet, what the long, quiet work of healing from complex trauma reveals is something far more detailed and deeply more liberating: the recognition that forgiveness is a cognitive act, while freedom is a somatic state, and the two are not nearly as related as we have been led to believe. The work isn't from the cognitive shoreline of understanding to the deep, cellular territory of the body, is not about finding a reason to pardon, but about reclaiming the nervous system from the echoes of the past.

The Cognitive Trap of Forgiveness

We are creatures of narrative, and the story of forgiveness is one of the most deeply embedded in our collective psyche. It is presented as a moral and psychological imperative, a final developmental milestone in the path of healing. We are told that to hold onto anger or resentment is to drink poison and expect the other person to die, a pithy and compelling aphorism that unfortunately collapses under the weight of complex trauma. This model presupposes a transactional reality, where an act of cognitive grace - the decision to forgive... somehow recalibrates the deep, non-verbal archives of the nervous system. But the body, as we know, keeps its own score, and it does not speak the language of shoulds and oughts. To demand that a system still braced for impact simply decide to be at peace is a fundamental misunderstanding of how trauma reorganizes our entire being. It places the burden of resolution on the very part of us, the thinking mind, that is often the least reliable in the face of overwhelming experience. The intellect can build beautiful, elaborate justifications for why forgiveness is the right and noble path, while the amygdala continues to sound the alarm, the heart continues to race, and the gut continues to clench. This creates a painful and confusing dissonance, a state of internal fragmentation where one part of the self is shaming another for failing to achieve a state it is not yet equipped to inhabit. It becomes another stick with which to beat oneself, another metric of failure in a life already defined by a sense of not measuring up. The pursuit of forgiveness can, in this way, become a subtle form of self-abandonment, a turning away from the raw, unvarnished truth of our own somatic reality in favor of a more palatable, socially acceptable story.

The Body as the Arbiter of Freedom

The entire conversation shifts when we move our attention from the courthouse of the mind to the wild, untamed terrain of the body. Freedom, in the context of C-PTSD, is not a verdict delivered by a jury of our peers or our own intellect; it is a felt sense. It is the palpable experience of being able to take a full breath, of feeling one’s feet on the ground, of sensing the boundaries of one’s own skin without a concurrent sense of dread. This is the area of the autonomic nervous system, the complex, unconscious network that is constantly scanning the environment for cues of safety and danger. It is here that the work of liberation truly happens. The pioneering work of researcher Stephen Porges and his Polyvagal Theory provides a map for this territory, explaining how our physiological state dictates our psychological experience. Porges introduced the concept of neuroception, the nervous system’s capacity to assess risk without the involvement of the conscious mind. For the survivor of complex trauma, this neuroceptive process is often exquisitely sensitized, biased toward detecting threat even in its absence. Freedom, then, is not about changing the past or forgiving the actors in it, but about updating this neuroceptive lens. It is about providing the nervous system with enough new, countervailing experiences of safety that it can begin to release its hypervigilant grip. This is not a cognitive process. You cannot talk your vagus nerve into a state of calm. As one of the most respected resources on trauma, the community at Psychology Today often highlights, the path to regulation is experiential. It is found in the rhythm of the breath, in the warmth of a compassionate gaze, in the grounding force of gravity. It is a slow, patient titration of presence, a gradual re-inhabiting of a body that was once a site of terror. The goal is not to forgive the unforgivable, but to become so rooted in the safety of the present moment that the past loses its power to hijack the nervous system.

I want to end with something that might sound strange. Gratitude for your symptoms. Not toxic positivity gratitude. Not "everything happens for a reason" gratitude. Real gratitude. For the parts of you that kept you alive. For the hypervigilance that spotted real danger. For the dissociation that protected you from unbearable pain. For the fawn response that kept you safe when fighting back wasn't an option.

Richard Schwartz says that when we approach our protector parts with gratitude instead of frustration, something shifts. They soften. They don't need to work so hard. Because they're finally being seen for what they are - not enemies, not pathology, but loyal soldiers who never got the message that the war is over.

So here's my invitation. The next time a symptom shows up - the anxiety, the numbness, the rage, the people-pleasing - before you try to fix it or fight it or shame yourself for it, try this. Say "thank you." Say "I know you're trying to help." Say "I see you." And then, gently, ask: "Is there something else you need me to know?" You might be surprised by the answer. You might be surprised by what becomes possible when you stop fighting yourself and start listening instead.

For understanding your attachment patterns in relationships, a popular choice is Attached (paid link).

When 'Letting Go' Becomes Another Burden

The Architecture of True Neutrality

In our cultural obsession with happiness and positivity, we have come to misunderstand the true nature of peace. We imagine it as a state of perpetual bliss, a constant feeling of lightness and joy. But for the nervous system, the ultimate state of safety is not positivity; it is neutrality. It is the absence of the alarm, the cessation of the hypervigilant scanning, the simple, unadorned experience of the present moment without the overlay of past or future. It turns out to be the promised land of healing. It is not a place of forced smiles or manufactured optimism, but a quiet, grounded state of being where the body is no longer at war with itself. This neutrality is the hallmark of a regulated ventral vagal state, the branch of our nervous system responsible for social engagement and connection. When this system is online, we are not necessarily “happy.” We are calm, we are present, we are open, we are available for connection. The world ceases to be a minefield of potential triggers and becomes, simply, the world. A car backfiring is just a sound, not a threat. This is a striking and radical shift, a move from a world colored by the constant threat of “what if” to a world grounded in the simple reality of “what is.” Achieving this state has nothing to do with forgiving the person who installed the minefield in the first place. It has everything to do with decommissioning the mines, one by one, through the patient, consistent application of awareness. It is the work of noticing the clench in the jaw and inviting it to soften, of noticing the shallow breath and allowing it to deepen, of noticing the impulse to flee and choosing to feel one’s feet on the floor instead. Each of these small, conscious acts is a vote for neutrality. It is a message sent to the deepest parts of the brain and body that the war is over, even if the memories of it remain. This is a more honest and attainable goal than the culturally imposed mandate of forgiveness. It does not ask us to lie to ourselves or to pretend that the harm did not matter. It asks only that we tend to our own internal field with enough care and precision that we can, at last, experience the significant peace of a system at rest. This process is central to understanding the principles of somatic work.

The Practice of Somatic Uncoupling

For a deeper understanding of how trauma lives in the body, I recommend The Body Keeps the Score by Bessel van der Kolk (paid link).

For those recovering from toxic relationships, you might find helpful Whole Again (paid link).

From Forgiveness to Freedom: A Shift in Gaze

Ultimately, the process out of complex trauma requires a radical shift in gaze - away from the perpetrator, away from the past, and toward the intimate, immediate reality of one’s own internal world. The culture teaches us to look outward for resolution, to seek apologies, to grant pardons, to find closure through some form of transaction with the other. But the body teaches us that resolution is an inside job. It is not about the other person at all. It is about our relationship with ourselves. It is about whether we are willing to turn toward the parts of us that are still frozen in time, still braced for impact, and offer them the one thing they have never had: a safe and compassionate witness. Forgiveness, with its focus on the other, can be a distraction from this deeper, more essential work. It keeps our attention fixed on the external event, on the person who harmed us, on the injustice of it all. While these are valid and important realities to acknowledge, they are not the territory of healing. The territory of healing is the body. It is the nervous system. It is the here and now. The question of forgiveness may resolve itself in time, or it may not. It may become irrelevant. What matters is not whether we can cognitively absolve another, but whether we can somatically liberate ourselves. Can we learn to inhabit our own skin with a sense of ease and belonging? Can we learn to trust the signals of our own body? Can we build a life that is not a reaction to the past, but an expression of our own deepest truth? That becomes the work. This is the path. It is a path that leads not to the hollow victory of forgiveness, but to the earned, embodied reality of freedom. It is a path that requires us to understand the layered dance of our own survival responses and to meet them not with judgment, but with striking respect and care. And for those navigating the complexities of dissociation, resources from the International Society for the Study of Trauma and Dissociation can be an invaluable guide. What happens when we stop demanding the story have a different ending and instead learn to write a new chapter from a place of inner safety?

I want to talk about something that doesn't get said enough in recovery spaces. Healing can feel like loss. When you start to let go of your survival strategies - the hypervigilance, the people-pleasing, the emotional walls - it can feel like you're losing yourself. Because those strategies were you, for a very long time. They were how you knew yourself.

Gabor Mate writes about this beautifully. The true self that got buried under survival adaptations - it's still there. But meeting it can be disorienting. Who am I without the anxiety? Who am I without the need to control everything? Who am I when I'm not performing for someone else's approval? These are terrifying questions. And they don't have quick answers.

But here's what I've found. On the other side of that disorientation is something real. Something that doesn't need to perform or protect or prove. Something that can just be. It's quiet. It's simple. And it's been waiting for you this whole time. You don't have to find it all at once. You just have to be willing to keep looking. Even when it's scary. Especially when it's scary.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link).

Let that land for a moment.

A guided journal specifically designed for trauma recovery is Trauma Recovery Journal (paid link).

If you are waiting for permission to start, here it is. If you are waiting for the right moment, this is it. If you are waiting to feel ready, you will wait forever. It is a decision. Make it.
